annotations.label: Prime-counting function π(x)

anchor.snippet: Let π( x ) be the prime-counting function that gives the number of primes less than or equal to x

annotations.note: Nat.primeCounting n is defined as the number of primes ≤ n, exactly the prime-counting function π.

annotations.label: π(10) = 4

anchor.snippet: For example, π(10) = 4 because there are four prime numbers

annotations.note: The function Nat.primeCounting exists and the value is decidable, but no evaluation lemma π(10)=4 is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prime number theorem (asymptotic law)

anchor.snippet: The prime number theorem then states that x / ln( x ) is a good approximation to π( x )

annotations.note: Mathlib has only Chebyshev-type upper/lower bounds on π(x); the asymptotic equivalence π(x) ~ x/ln x is not in Mathlib (it lives in the external PrimeNumberTheoremAnd project).

annotations.label: Legendre's conjecture for π(a)

anchor.snippet: Adrien-Marie Legendre conjectured in 1797 or 1798 that π( a ) is approximated by the function

annotations.note: Legendre's approximation π(a) ≈ a/(ln a − B) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirichlet's theorem over Gaussian integers

anchor.snippet: In 1841 he generalized his arithmetic progressions theorem from integers to the ring of Gaussian integers

annotations.note: Dirichlet's theorem is formalized only for the integers (primes in residue classes mod q), not for the ring of Gaussian integers.

annotations.label: Chebyshev: limit must equal one

anchor.snippet: he succeeded in proving a slightly weaker form of the asymptotic law

annotations.note: Chebyshev's result that the limit of π(x)/(x/ln x), if it exists, must equal 1 is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chebyshev bounds on π(x)/(x/ln x)

anchor.snippet: He was able to prove unconditionally that this ratio is bounded above and below by two explicitly given constants

annotations.note: Explicit Chebyshev upper bound (pi_le_log4_mul_div / eventually_primeCounting_le) and lower bound (Chebyshev.pi_ge) on the prime-counting function are both proved.

annotations.label: Bertrand's postulate

anchor.snippet: prove Bertrand's postulate that there exists a prime number between n and 2 n for any integer n ≥ 2

annotations.note: Nat.exists_prime_lt_and_le_two_mul proves for n ≠ 0 there is a prime p with n < p ≤ 2n, exactly Bertrand's postulate.

annotations.label: Riemann hypothesis (conjecture)

anchor.snippet: one of which is the well-known Riemann hypothesis

annotations.note: The statement of the Riemann hypothesis is formalized as the Prop definition RiemannHypothesis (unproven).

annotations.label: Non-vanishing of ζ(s) on Re(s)=1

anchor.snippet: the Riemann zeta function ζ( s ) is non-zero for all complex values of the variable s

annotations.note: riemannZeta_ne_zero_of_one_le_re proves ζ(s)≠0 for all Re(s) ≥ 1, which includes the line Re(s)=1.

annotations.label: Goldston–Pintz–Yıldırım bounded gaps result

anchor.snippet: which they originally used to prove that

annotations.note: The GPY small-gaps-between-primes result is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclid: infinitely many primes

anchor.snippet: Euclid showed that there are infinitely many prime numbers

annotations.note: Nat.exists_infinite_primes (also Nat.infinite_setOf_prime) is Euclid's theorem on the infinitude of primes.

annotations.label: Gauss's conjecture (logarithmic integral)

anchor.snippet: conjectured that the number of primes less than or equal to a large number N is close to the value of the integral

annotations.note: The logarithmic integral Li and the approximation π(N) ≈ Li(N) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prime number theorem

anchor.snippet: This remarkable result is what is now known as the prime number theorem

annotations.note: Only Chebyshev bounds exist in Mathlib; the full prime number theorem is not in Mathlib (it is in the external PrimeNumberTheoremAnd project).

annotations.label: Dirichlet's theorem on arithmetic progressions

anchor.snippet: Dirichlet proved that any arithmetic progression with a and q coprime contains infinitely many primes

annotations.note: Nat.infinite_setOf_prime_and_eq_mod proves infinitely many primes p ≡ a (mod q) for a a unit, exactly Dirichlet's theorem.

anchors.snippet: Dirichlet proved that any arithmetic progression with a and q coprime contains infinitely many primes

anchors.value: {\displaystyle \pi (x,a,q)=({\text{number of primes }}\leq x{\text{ in the arithmetic progression }}a+nq,\ n\in \mathbf {Z} ),}

annotations.label: PNT for arithmetic progressions

anchor.snippet: The prime number theorem can be generalised to this problem

annotations.note: The prime number theorem for arithmetic progressions (asymptotic count of primes in a residue class) is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The prime number theorem can be generalised to this problem

anchors.value: {\displaystyle \pi (x,a,q)=({\text{number of primes }}\leq x{\text{ in the arithmetic progression }}a+nq,\ n\in \mathbf {Z} ),}

annotations.label: Twin prime conjecture

anchor.snippet: the twin prime conjecture which asks whether there are infinitely many primes p such that p + 2 is prime

annotations.note: No twin-prime statement or definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bounded gaps (k ≤ 12 under Elliott–Halberstam)

anchor.snippet: On the assumption of the Elliott–Halberstam conjecture it has been proven recently that there are infinitely many primes p such that p + k is prime for some positive even k at most 12

annotations.note: The conditional bounded-gaps result (k ≤ 12 under Elliott–Halberstam) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bounded gaps (k ≤ 246 unconditional)

anchor.snippet: it has been proven unconditionally (i.e. not depending on unproven conjectures) that there are infinitely many primes p such that p + k is prime for some positive even k at most 246

annotations.note: The unconditional bounded-gaps theorem (k ≤ 246, Zhang–Polymath) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Waring's problem

anchor.snippet: Waring's problem , which asks whether it is possible, for any k ≥ 2, to write any positive integer as the sum of a bounded number of k th powers

annotations.note: Only the squares case (Lagrange's four-square theorem) is formalized; the general Waring problem for arbitrary k is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lagrange's four-square theorem

anchor.snippet: Lagrange in 1770, who proved that every positive integer is the sum of at most four squares

annotations.note: Nat.sum_four_squares proves every n is a²+b²+c²+d², exactly Lagrange's four-square theorem.

annotations.label: Hilbert's solution of Waring's problem

anchor.snippet: The general case was proved by Hilbert in 1909, using algebraic techniques which gave no explicit bounds

annotations.note: Hilbert's general solution of Waring's problem (existence of g(k) for all k) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vinogradov's bound for G(k)

anchor.snippet: give explicit upper bounds for the function G ( k ), the smallest number of k th powers needed, such as Vinogradov 's bound

annotations.note: Vinogradov's upper bound for the Waring function G(k) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss circle problem

anchor.snippet: the Gauss circle problem , which asks for integers points ( x y ) which satisfy

annotations.note: The Gauss circle problem (counting lattice points inside a circle) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lattice point count with error term

anchor.snippet: It is not hard to prove that the answer is

annotations.note: The lattice-point count N(r) = πr² + E(r) with error term is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss's error bound

anchor.snippet: It was shown by Gauss that

annotations.note: Gauss's error bound E(r) = O(r) for the circle problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sierpiński's 1906 bound

anchor.snippet: The first to attain this was Sierpiński in 1906, who showed

annotations.note: Sierpiński's O(r^(2/3)) error bound for the Gauss circle problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hardy–Landau lower bound

anchor.snippet: In 1915, Hardy and Landau each showed that one does not have

annotations.note: The Hardy–Landau Omega lower bound on the circle-problem error term is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Huxley's 2000 result

anchor.snippet: In 2000 Huxley showed

annotations.note: Huxley's 2000 exponent bound for the Gauss circle problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirichlet series

anchor.snippet: Dirichlet series , which are functions of a complex variable defined by an infinite series of the form

annotations.note: LSeries f s = ∑ f n / n^s is exactly the Dirichlet series of the coefficient sequence f.

annotations.label: Convergence on a half-plane

anchor.snippet: this series may converge everywhere, nowhere, or on some half plane

annotations.note: LSeries.abscissaOfAbsConv plus LSeriesSummable_of_abscissaOfAbsConv_lt_re formalize the abscissa of (absolute) convergence and the half-plane of convergence.

annotations.label: Product of Dirichlet series as convolution

anchor.snippet: hence the coefficients of the product of two Dirichlet series are the multiplicative convolutions of the original coefficients

annotations.note: LSeries_convolution proves L(f ⍟ g) = L(f)·L(g), i.e. the product corresponds to Dirichlet convolution of coefficients.

annotations.label: Euler product for ζ(s)

anchor.snippet: Euler showed that the fundamental theorem of arithmetic implies (at least formally) the Euler product

annotations.note: riemannZeta_eulerProduct (and its tprod/HasProd variants) gives ζ(s) = ∏_p (1 − p^{−s})^{−1} for Re(s) > 1.

annotations.label: Euler's analytic proof of infinitude of primes

anchor.snippet: Euler's proof of the infinity of prime numbers makes use of the divergence of the term at the left hand side for s = 1

annotations.note: not_summable_one_div_on_primes (divergence of the sum of prime reciprocals) is the analytic infinitude-of-primes result underlying Euler's argument and is strictly stronger than mere infinitude.

annotations.label: Meromorphic continuation of ζ(s)

anchor.snippet: this function can be extended to a meromorphic function on the entire plane with a simple pole at s = 1

annotations.note: riemannZeta is the entire analytic continuation differentiable away from 1 (differentiableAt_riemannZeta), with a simple pole at s=1 given by riemannZeta_residue_one.

annotations.label: Riemann Hypothesis (non-trivial zeros)

anchor.snippet: Riemann conjectured that all the "non-trivial" zeros of ζ lie on the line

annotations.note: The Prop RiemannHypothesis states exactly that non-trivial zeros of ζ have real part 1/2 (statement only, unproven).

annotations.label: Error term in PNT under RH

anchor.snippet: under the assumption of the Riemann Hypothesis, the error term in the prime number theorem

annotations.note: The RH-conditional error term π(x) = Li(x) + O(√x log x) in the prime number theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hardy: infinitely many zeros on critical line

anchor.snippet: in 1914, Hardy proved that there were infinitely many zeros of the zeta function on the critical line

annotations.note: Hardy's theorem on infinitely many zeros on the critical line is not in Mathlib; ZetaZeros.lean only proves discreteness of the zero set.
